title: Mud on Cotton
stainChemistry: Mud is soil particles suspended in water — clay + silt + sand + some organic matter (plant debris, microbes). The particles bond to cotton mechanically, not chemically; water helps them settle between fibers. The #1 rule: LET IT DRY COMPLETELY first. Wet mud grinds deeper and spreads when touched; dry mud flakes off cleanly with a brush. Cotton tolerates NSD, enzymes (for any organic fraction), 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash at room temperature. Most of the 'cleanup' is mechanical.
whyThisWorks: Drying + brushing off removes 80–90% of the mud physically, no chemistry needed. What remains is the fine-particle residue plus any organic tint from plant debris or soil microbes. NSD + warm water handles that residue; enzyme pretreat covers any organic component; peroxide clears any residual earth-tone shadow on whites. Resist the urge to rinse fresh mud — it makes the stain bigger.
defaultAssumption: Mud on cotton is one of the easiest common stains if the dry-first rule is followed. The typical home-treatment failure is rinsing wet mud immediately, which spreads and grinds particles into the weave.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay the garment flat in a well-ventilated spot. Let the mud dry completely — do NOT rinse, dab, or rub it while wet. Dry mud flakes off; wet mud smears.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Stiff-bristle brush or an old toothbrush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Over a trash can or outside, brush the dried mud off with a stiff brush. Work against the weave to lift particles. Tap the fabric to shake loose dust. 80–90% of the mud leaves in this step alone.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Clears residual fine-particle soil and any light organic tint.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Enzyme pretreat OR Liquid Protein Spotter if heavy organic
spottingProtocol.instruction: If mud contained grass, leaf fragments, or microbial residue (common in yard mud), an enzyme pretreat handles the organic component. Skip if the mud was clean clay / sand.

steps: 1. Let mud dry completely — never treat wet.
4. Enzyme pretreat if organic (grass, leaves) was in the mud.
professionalProtocol.temperature: dry through mechanical phase; warm NSD; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never rinse wet mud — spreads particles + grinds into weave
Brush outside or over a trash can — fine dust scatters
Red clay / iron-rich mud may leave a rust-like tint; treat with a dilute acid rinse if needed

homeSolutions.agent: Let the Mud Dry — Don't Rinse It
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the single most important rule for mud: LEAVE IT ALONE until it's completely dry. Do NOT rinse, dab, or scrub wet mud — that spreads the particles and grinds them into the fabric. Hang the garment flat somewhere ventilated and wait 1–4 hours. Patience here saves you the hardest part.

Take the garment outside (or over a trash can). Use a stiff-bristle brush or an old toothbrush and brush the dried mud off in short strokes against the weave. Tap the fabric to shake loose fine dust. Most of the mud comes off here — you'll be surprised how much.
What's left is fine-particle residue plus maybe a faint earth-tone shadow. Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush, rinse warm. Repeat if a shadow remains.
If a brown shadow is still visible after the Dawn pass (often from organic matter in yard mud), dissolve 1 scoop OxiClean in 1 cup warm water, soak the spot 15–30 min, rinse.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide — one oxidizer at a time.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If any shadow remains, repeat Dawn / OxiClean first.

If the mud was red clay (Southeastern US soil, for instance), there may be a rust-like tint from iron content. Dab a few drops of lemon juice or dilute white vinegar on it for 3 minutes, rinse cool. The mild acid converts the iron to a soluble form. Re-wash if needed.

Mud rarely requires a professional — dry-and-brush handles most cases. If the mud has been run through a hot dryer (particles locked into the fiber) and two home rounds didn't clear it, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Mud on cotton, age [fresh / dried / washed-through], home-treated with dry brush + Dawn + OxiClean + [lemon juice for red clay]."

steps: 1. LET MUD DRY completely — do not rinse, dab, or scrub wet mud.
2. Brush + tap dried mud off outside or over a trash can.
4. OxiClean soak 15–30 min if brown shadow remains.
5. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
6. Red clay: extra lemon juice / vinegar step for iron tint.
products: Lemon juice OR white vinegar (red clay only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if mud has been hot-dried into the fiber and home chemistry doesn't clear the shadow.
warnings: NEVER rinse wet mud — spreads and grinds
Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach or hydrogen peroxide
Red clay iron content may need a separate acid step

neverDo: Never rinse or rub wet mud — grinds into weave
Never skip the brush-off — skipping doubles the chemistry work
Never hot-dry until the mud is fully gone
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, enzymes, OxiClean, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash
Dyed cotton: no peroxide needed usually

materialWarnings: Red clay has iron — separate treatment

escalation.when: Mud has been hot-dried into the fabric AND home round didn't clear it (rare).
escalation.whatToTell: "Mud on cotton, age [fresh / dried / washed-through], home-treated with dry brush + Dawn + OxiClean + [lemon juice for red clay]."

contextQuestions: Is the mud fresh or dried already?
What kind of mud — garden / yard / red clay / construction?
Has it been through a hot dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Mud is the stain where PATIENCE > chemistry. Drying + brushing is the whole game. Red clay's iron content is the only real variant that changes treatment.
